mnemonics.title: All Pigs Eat Diamonds
mnemonics.content: For SPD matrices: All (all eigenvalues > 0), Pigs (Positive definite), Eat (Eigenvalues), Diamonds (Definitely positive). Remember: A symmetric matrix is positive definite if and only if ALL its eigenvalues are positive.

mnemonics.title: Markov's MAGIC Rule
mnemonics.content: MAGIC = Matrix (stochastic), All rows sum to 1, Greater than or equal to zero, In (each entry between 0 and 1), Column stochastic variant. A Markov matrix has non-negative entries where each row sums to 1, representing transition probabilities.

mnemonics.title: xTAx: Turn Around eXamine
mnemonics.content: xTAx > 0: Turn (transpose) the vector, Around (multiply A), eXamine (check if result is positive). For positive definite: x^T A x > 0 for ALL non-zero x. Think: 'Turn around and examine the quadratic form!'

mnemonics.title: Eigenvalues: Enter, Exit, Transform
mnemonics.content: For positive definite: Eigenvalues Enter (positive numbers) and stay positive. For Markov: Eigenvalue = 1 always exists (Exit = 1), and |λ| ≤ 1 for all eigenvalues.

mnemonics.title: Cholesky: Choose Logical Y
mnemonics.content: Cholesky Decomposition: A = LL^T where L is lower triangular with positive diagonal entries. Choose (Cholesky), Logical (Lower), Y (L looks like upside-down Y). Only works for SPD matrices!

acronyms.usage: Used in optimization (convex functions), machine learning (kernels), and numerical analysis. Always has positive eigenvalues and can be decomposed as A = LL^T.
Matrix where x^T A x > 0 for all non-zero x. Used in quadratic optimization, ensuring unique minima exist.
For SPD matrices, singular values = eigenvalues. Used in PCA, dimensionality reduction. SVD always produces orthogonal matrices.
States dominant eigenvalue of Markov matrix has positive eigenvector. Used in PageRank and stationary distributions.
Efficient for SPD matrices. L is lower triangular with positive diagonals. Used in solving linear systems, Monte Carlo simulation.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bowl (paraboloid) opening upward. The matrix A defines the curvature. If the bowl never touches the ground (always positive), it's positive definite. Draw x^T A x as the height at point x - it should always be above ground (positive).

Draw a state diagram with circles (states) and arrows (transitions). Each row shows probabilities leaving a state. The row sum = 1 means all arrows from a state account for 100% probability. Think of water flowing through pipes - total water is conserved.

For 2x2 matrix, imagine stretching a unit circle into an ellipse. The eigenvalues = scaling factors in principal directions. Positive definite = ellipse stays in first quadrant (no flipping/inversion). Markov: one eigenvalue = 1 (fixed scaling in probability direction).

Think of a Markov chain as a leaky bucket system. Stationary distribution = water level that stays constant when you keep adding and removing water at the same rate. π = πP where P is the Markov matrix.

Positive definite Hessian = smooth bowl with single bottom. Draw contour lines as concentric ellipses (for SPD). Negative definite = bowl opening downward (maximum). Indefinite = saddle shape (both up and down directions).

analogies.analogy: Think of a stable spring. When you compress or stretch it (apply vector x), the stored energy (x^T A x) is always positive unless you don't move it (x=0). Like a well-built trampoline - it always bounces back up, never collapses.

Think of a city subway system. Each station (state) has trains going to other stations. The Markov matrix tells you the probability of taking each route. All routes from a station must account for 100% of departures - you always go somewhere!

Imagine a rotating merry-go-round. Eigenvalue 1 means the distribution of people (probability) doesn't change shape - it just rotates or stays the same. The total number of people (sum = 1) is always preserved.

Like breaking a square into two triangles. A SPD matrix is like a perfect square, and Cholesky is finding the right-angled triangle (L) that reconstructs it. Much easier to work with triangular matrices!

Think of it as a score in a game. You have a team (vector x) and a strategy (matrix A). The score (x^T A x) tells you how well the strategy works. Positive definite = always a positive score unless your team is empty (x=0).
